title: Four-shaft bird's-eye twill scarf
subtitle: A 180 × 30 cm scarf in 2/20 merino warp and weft, woven in bird's-eye twill on four shafts
excerpt: Bird's-eye twill produces small diamond floats on a twill ground, giving the cloth a subtle, repeating eye-like texture. Worked in fine merino at 24 EPI, the finished scarf drapes well and shows the pattern clearly.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from standard four-shaft twill weaving references and public-domain weave structure documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: The set of threads stretched lengthwise on the loom under tension, through which the weft is interlaced.
The thread carried horizontally across the warp on each pick (row) of weaving.
The number of warp threads per unit of width, stated in ends per inch (EPI) or ends per centimetre.
A weave structure where the weft crosses over two or more warp threads in a staggered diagonal pattern, producing diagonal lines on the cloth face.
The sequence in which warp threads are drawn through the heddles on each shaft, determining the weave structure.
The sequence in which the weaver presses the treadles to raise specific shafts and form each shed.
A looped wire or cord on a shaft that holds a warp thread and raises or lowers it when the shaft moves.
The V-shaped opening between raised and lowered warp threads through which the shuttle passes.
A single pass of the weft through the shed.
A warp or weft thread that spans over two or more threads without interlacing, creating a longer exposed length on the cloth surface.
Washing and pressing the woven cloth off the loom to set the fibres, even out the weave, and achieve the intended hand.
The reduction in warp length caused by the interlacing of weft threads as weaving progresses, typically expressed as a percentage of the total warp.

content.text: Before the draft, here is a plain-English description of the structure. In a
weave, each weft thread crosses over a group of warp threads in a sequence that steps one position diagonally on each row. Bird's-eye twill is a four-shaft twill variation where the
alternates in a way that creates small, isolated
spots on the twill ground. The result looks like small diamond or eye shapes across the cloth surface.
This scarf finishes at approximately 180 × 30 cm. The yarn is 2/20 merino,
in the width and 8% shrinkage in the length after
. Warp to a length of approximately 230 cm (including 40 cm loom waste) and a width of 33 cm in the reed. Total warp ends: 33 cm × 9.4 ends/cm = approximately 310 ends. Use the same yarn for the
, or choose a contrasting colour for the weft to emphasise the bird's-eye pattern.

items.name: Four-shaft floor loom with at least 35 cm weaving width

content.text: is a straight draw: 1, 2, 3, 4, repeating across the full warp width. The tie-up is a standard 2/2 balanced twill: each treadle lifts two adjacent shafts. Treadle 1 lifts shafts 1 and 2; treadle 2 lifts shafts 2 and 3; treadle 3 lifts shafts 3 and 4; treadle 4 lifts shafts 4 and 1. The treadling follows 1, 2, 3, 4 in a straight repeat, which produces the bird's-eye pattern where the threading and treadling intersect to create small isolated floats.
Wind 310 ends on the warping board to a length of 230 cm. Beam the warp under firm, even tension. Thread each end through the

on the correct shaft following the 1-2-3-4 straight draw, working right to left across the full width. Sley the 12-dent reed at 24 EPI: 2 ends per dent throughout. Tie onto the front apron rod in small, even bouts.
Weave 3 cm of plain weave as a header to spread the warp and stabilise the selvedges before beginning the bird's-eye treadling sequence.
with treadle 1 (shafts 1 and 2 rise). Pass the shuttle through and beat to a
count matching the sett: approximately 9 picks per cm for a balanced twill at 24 EPI. Maintain an even, consistent beat throughout.

Treadle sequence: 1, 2, 3, 4, repeating for the full length of the scarf. The bird's-eye pattern becomes visible after 8 to 10 picks. Keep selvedges neat: at 24 EPI with fine merino, the thread is slippery. Wrap the weft around the outermost warp thread at each selvedge before beating. Continue until the woven length on the loom reaches approximately 195 cm (to account for take-up and shrinkage to 180 cm finished).

Weave 3 cm of plain weave to close the scarf and work a hemstitch at both ends in groups of 4 warp ends before cutting from the loom. Trim fringe to an even 4 cm if a fringe finish is wanted, or knot in groups of 4 and trim close to the knot for a neat end.

Wet finish by hand-washing in warm water (30 °C) with a small amount of wool wash. Do not agitate: submerge, press gently, and leave to soak for 5 minutes. Rinse at the same temperature. Roll in a towel to extract moisture, then lay flat. While still damp, press with a warm iron (wool setting) and a pressing cloth. The scarf will soften and the twill structure will consolidate.

items.solution: Check that the threading is a strict 1-2-3-4 straight draw with no threading errors. A single skipped or repeated shaft disrupts the pattern. Also check that the treadling is in the correct 1-2-3-4 sequence without gaps.
The weft arc is too tight. Lay the weft in a wider arc before beating. At 24 EPI, fine merino needs a generous arc to avoid draw-in.

items.problem: Floats on the cloth surface are longer than expected
items.solution: This indicates the treadling sequence has been disrupted. Check the treadle order. If treadles are pressed in the wrong sequence, the twill steps incorrectly and creates longer floats.
2/20 merino is a fine yarn and can abrade at the heddles under high tension. Ease the warp tension slightly and check the heddles for rough edges or burrs.
